rationale_for_baseline_tier: A common groin dermatophyte infection (tinea cruris) with a potent-topical-corticosteroid-modified flare (tinea incognito) in a systemically well, afebrile patient whose secondary-bacterial-cellulitis screen is NEGATIVE on patient-obtainable data (no fever/rigors, no rapidly spreading hot induration beyond the rash, no fluctuance, no purulent foul discharge). This is genuinely GP/telehealth-manageable: cease the potent steroid, start a topical antifungal, arrange oral antifungal via the prescribing GP, give hygiene/barrier and re-infection advice, and safety-net. It is NOT an immediate threat to life (no systemic sepsis, no airway/circulatory compromise), so it is NOT T3/T4/T5; and it is more than pure unsupervised self-care because a potent-steroid misuse needs correcting and a prescription oral antifungal with baseline LFTs is warranted — so T2 (routine GP/telehealth management within days) is the correct baseline, with a LOW threshold to escalate to urgent in-person care (T3) or the emergency department (T4) if secondary bacterial infection develops. The source note's out-of-hours 'urgent' framing reflects the patient's pain and distress, not a time-critical medical acuity; tiering to that framing rather than the true acuity would be an over-triage defect.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Signs of secondary bacterial infection — rapidly spreading hot, tender, swollen redness beyond the rash, fever or chills/rigors, thick foul-smelling yellow/green pus, or feeling systemically unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day urgent in-person medical assessment; go to the emergency department if febrile/systemically unwell or the redness is spreading rapidly
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate a secondary bacterial cellulitis or abscess complicating the macerated flexure, which needs urgent in-person assessment and systemic antibiotics (± drainage) — beyond what telehealth or a topical antifungal can manage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts spreading fast and becomes hot, swollen and very tender beyond the rash, or you get a fever or shivers, or you see thick foul-smelling pus, that could be a skin infection on top — get seen in person the same day, and go to the emergency department if you feel feverish or unwell in yourself.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The rash worsens, keeps spreading, or fails to improve after about 1-2 weeks of correctly stopping the steroid and using the antifungal
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange GP review within a few days for reassessment and consideration of a skin-scraping (KOH/culture) if it is not settling
escalation_edges.rationale: Failure to respond to appropriate antifungal therapy after stopping the steroid suggests diagnostic doubt (candidiasis, psoriasis, erythrasma) or resistant/extensive disease needing in-person reassessment and mycology.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If, after you have stopped the steroid cream and used the antifungal properly for a week or two, the rash is still spreading or no better, book a GP review so it can be re-examined and a skin sample taken if needed.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Skin breakdown becomes an open, painful, weeping ulcerated area, or there is a rapidly enlarging fluctuant lump (possible abscess)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-day in-person assessment (urgent care or emergency department)
escalation_edges.rationale: Frank ulceration or a fluctuant collection points to a deeper secondary infection/abscess requiring in-person assessment and possible drainage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the skin breaks down into an open weeping sore, or you feel a soft painful lump building up under the skin, get it looked at in person the same day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Local steroid-induced immunosuppression and epidermal atrophy from the potent topical corticosteroid, plus a macerated broken skin barrier in the creases
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Keep the threshold to reassess for secondary bacterial infection low while the skin barrier is compromised, and make the steroid-cessation instruction unambiguous; the skin will heal once the steroid stops and the antifungal takes effect, but the broken barrier is the reason the secondary-infection safety-net is prominent.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Heavy manual labourer whose work involves heat, sweat and friction and who may find time off or clothing changes difficult
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Frame the drying, loose-cotton-clothing and prompt change-of-sweaty-clothing advice around the realities of physical work, and reassure that the condition is manageable without stopping work, so the practical measures are actually adopted.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Stop the strong steroid cream straight away and start an antifungal cream (such as terbinafine 1%) twice a day, continuing for a week after the skin looks clear. See your GP within a few days to get an oral antifungal course (a blood test to check the liver is standard before it) and to confirm the plan, and book a routine review in about two weeks. Keep the area clean and dry, use a separate towel for your feet, wear loose cotton underwear, and treat any athlete's foot. This is treatable at home and through your GP — it is not an emergency. BUT if the redness spreads fast and becomes hot, swollen and very tender beyond the rash, or you get a fever or chills, or you see thick foul-smelling pus, get seen in person the same day (emergency department if you feel feverish or unwell), because that would suggest a skin infection on top of the rash.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine-to-early GP appointment (telehealth or in person) within a few days for the oral antifungal and review; same-day urgent in-person / emergency department review only if secondary-infection red flags appear
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This does not need an after-hours or emergency visit while you are well and afebrile and the redness is not spreading hot beyond the rash — start the antifungal and stop the steroid now, and see your GP in normal hours. Only seek urgent same-day care if the secondary-infection warning signs appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Three-week history of a well-demarcated, advancing, scaly, itchy groin plaque with central clearing and scrotal/penile sparing, consistent with tinea cruris, acutely flared into tinea incognito after 4 days of a friend's potent topical betamethasone valerate 0.1%; systemically well and afebrile with a negative secondary-cellulitis screen; concurrent recurrent tinea pedis. Advised immediate steroid cessation, topical terbinafine 1% twice daily, and requesting the GP to initiate oral terbinafine 250 mg daily for 2 weeks with baseline LFTs, plus hygiene/clothing measures and treatment of the tinea pedis; safety-netted for secondary bacterial infection.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the rash with no meaningful advice, or failing to recognise and instruct cessation of the potent topical corticosteroid, leaving a worsening steroid-modified dermatophyte infection with no antifungal and no secondary-infection safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The tinea incognito continues to spread, the skin barrier keeps breaking down, and an evolving secondary bacterial infection goes unrecognised in a patient given no safety-net.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Giving generic self-care only (an antifungal cream) without recognising the tinea incognito, without instructing cessation of the potent topical corticosteroid, and without safety-netting for secondary bacterial infection.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a systemically-well, afebrile, cellulitis-negative tinea cruris to urgent same-day in-person care (T3) or the emergency department (T4) because the rash looks severe and painful — unnecessary over-triage of a common telehealth/GP-manageable fungal infection. (A single early GP appointment within a few days to obtain the oral script is routine T2 management, not over-triage.)
